title: Sandbox, and the View from the Present
lesson:
You've arrived. The LC-3 is where the journey comes home: it's the very ISA in your UT Austin coursework, and you can now read it fluently. Look back at the road. The Manchester Baby (1948) proved a program could live in memory: a wall of valves, 128 bytes, no way even to add, 52 minutes for one calculation. The PDP-8 (1965) made computers buyable and gave them input and output. The 6502 (1976) put a CPU on a $25 chip and made computing personal. And the LC-3 distills all of it into a clean machine designed to teach the next generation, which is you.

Where does the road go from here? Two directions. Upward in abstraction: the C, Python, and everything you write compiles down to instructions exactly like these. And outward in scale: when one processor isn't enough, you connect tens of thousands of them. That's a supercomputer, and some of the most powerful on Earth, TACC's Frontera and Vista, are humming right now a short walk across the Forty Acres. From a tonne of valves in Manchester to a top-tier supercomputer in your own backyard. Hook 'em. 🤘
prompt: Have a play. The whole LC-3 is yours. Print messages with PUTS, build loops and subroutines (JSR and RET), walk arrays with LDR. You've earned it.
starterCode:
; Sandbox: no goal here. The LC-3 is yours to explore.
; This prints a message using a subroutine (JSR and RET).
.ORIG x3000
        LEA R0, MSG
        JSR PRINT      ; call the print routine
        HALT

; PRINT: prints the string whose address is in R0
PRINT   PUTS
        RET            ; return to the caller (uses R7)

MSG     .STRINGZ "HOOK EM"
.END
